title: Neural Networks
subtitle: The Brain Inside the Machine!
overview: This session takes a deep dive into neural networks — the architecture powering modern AI. Students explore how neurons, weights, activation functions, and backpropagation work together to let machines learn. From a single perceptron to multi-layer deep networks, students build intuition through analogies, visuals, and a hands-on Keras model.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — neuron diagrams, weight visualisations, activation function graphs, backpropagation flow, Keras model summary screenshot.

keyTopics: Biological vs Artificial Neuron — Dendrites → inputs, Cell body → summation, Axon → output, Synapse → weight
Perceptron — The simplest neural network unit: inputs × weights + bias → activation → output
Weights & Biases — Numbers the network adjusts during training to improve predictions
Activation Functions — ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh — adding non-linearity so networks learn complex patterns
Multi-Layer Network — Input layer, hidden layers, output layer — depth = ability to learn complex features
Forward Pass — Data flows from input to output through all layers to produce a prediction
Backpropagation — Error flows backward through the network, adjusting weights using gradients
Keras & TensorFlow — Python libraries to build, train, and evaluate neural networks in just a few lines

activity.title: Handwritten Digit Recogniser — Build a Neural Network with Keras on MNIST
parts: MNIST dataset (built into Keras — no download needed)
Whiteboard to draw the network architecture before coding
steps: Open Google Colab and create a new notebook
Import libraries: import tensorflow as tf, from tensorflow import keras, import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
Load the MNIST dataset: (X_train, y_train), (X_test, y_test) = keras.datasets.mnist.load_data()
Visualise a sample: plt.imshow(X_train[0], cmap='gray') — see the handwritten digit the network will learn to read
Normalise the data: X_train = X_train / 255.0 and X_test = X_test / 255.0 — discuss: why divide by 255?
Build the model: keras.Sequential with Flatten layer, Dense(128, activation='relu'), Dense(10, activation='softmax')
Train: model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=5) — watch accuracy climb each epoch!
Evaluate: model.evaluate(X_test, y_test) — see final accuracy on unseen test digits
Challenge: Visualise 5 predictions — show the digit image, predicted label, and confidence percentage side by side!

quiz.q: What part of a biological neuron does a WEIGHT represent in an artificial neural network?
options: The cell body that processes signals
The axon that carries the output signal
The synapse — the connection strength between neurons that determines how much influence one neuron has on the next
The dendrite that receives input signals
quiz.explanation: Weight = synapse strength! In the brain, strong synapses pass signals easily; weak ones barely pass anything. In a neural network, a high weight means that input has strong influence on the output. Training adjusts these weights!

options: A type of activation function used in deep networks
The simplest artificial neural network — a single neuron that takes weighted inputs, adds a bias, applies an activation, and produces an output
A layer in a deep neural network that processes images
A Python library for building neural networks
quiz.explanation: Perceptron = one neuron! It multiplies each input by its weight, adds them all up, adds a bias, then passes the sum through an activation function. The output is the prediction. Stack many perceptrons in layers = deep network!

quiz.q: What is the role of a BIAS in a neural network neuron?
options: It prevents the network from making any predictions
It stores the training data inside the neuron
It shifts the activation function left or right — allowing the neuron to fire even when all inputs are zero
It controls the learning rate of the network
quiz.explanation: Bias = the neuron's personal offset! Without bias, a neuron can only fire based on its inputs. Bias lets the neuron activate even when inputs are zero — giving the network more flexibility to fit any pattern.

quiz.q: What does the RELU activation function do?
options: Outputs a value between 0 and 1 — useful for probabilities
Outputs a value between -1 and 1 — centred around zero
Outputs the input directly if it is positive, and outputs zero if it is negative
Randomly drops neurons during training to prevent overfitting
quiz.explanation: ReLU = Rectified Linear Unit! f(x) = max(0, x). Negative input → output 0. Positive input → output as-is. Simple, fast, and works brilliantly in hidden layers. Most common activation function in modern deep learning!

quiz.q: What is the SIGMOID activation function best used for?
options: Hidden layers in deep networks
The output layer of a binary classification model — it squashes output to a probability between 0 and 1
Normalising input data before training
Calculating the loss during backpropagation
quiz.explanation: Sigmoid = probability squasher! Output is always between 0 and 1 — perfect for binary classification output (is this email spam? 0.92 → yes, probably!). Rarely used in hidden layers today — ReLU is faster and avoids vanishing gradients.

quiz.q: What is the SOFTMAX activation function used for?
options: Hidden layers to add non-linearity
Regression output layers to predict continuous values
The output layer of a multi-class classification model — converts raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1.0
Normalising weights during training
quiz.explanation: Softmax = multi-class probability distributor! If classifying digits 0–9, Softmax outputs 10 probabilities that sum to 1.0. The highest probability is the prediction — e.g. [0.02, 0.01, 0.91, ...] → predicted class is 2!

quiz.q: What is a FORWARD PASS in a neural network?
options: The process of updating weights using gradients
The process of loading training data into the network
Data flowing from the input layer through all hidden layers to the output layer to produce a prediction
Testing the model on the validation dataset
quiz.explanation: Forward pass = input to output journey! Data enters the input layer → passes through hidden layers (each applies weights + activation) → arrives at output layer as a prediction. Happens millions of times during training!

options: Running the model backwards to undo a bad prediction
Loading data in reverse order to improve training speed
The algorithm that calculates how much each weight contributed to the prediction error and adjusts them to reduce the loss
A method of visualising the network architecture
quiz.explanation: Backpropagation = the teacher correcting the network! After a forward pass, the error (loss) is calculated. Backprop flows this error backwards through the network, computing gradients for every weight. Optimiser then nudges each weight to reduce the error!

options: A technique for visualising neural network layers
An optimisation algorithm that adjusts weights in the direction that reduces the loss — like rolling a ball downhill to find the lowest point
A method for splitting data into training and testing sets
A type of regularisation to prevent overfitting
quiz.explanation: Gradient Descent = rolling downhill to minimum loss! Imagine the loss as a hilly landscape. The gradient points uphill. Move in the OPPOSITE direction (downhill) and the loss decreases. Repeat millions of times → weights converge to good values!

quiz.q: What is an EPOCH in neural network training?
options: A single neuron in the output layer
One complete pass through the ENTIRE training dataset — all training examples seen once
The learning rate used by the optimiser
The number of layers in a deep neural network
quiz.explanation: Epoch = one full school day! In one epoch, the network sees every single training example once, makes predictions, calculates loss, and updates weights via backprop. Training for 10 epochs = seeing the full dataset 10 times!

quiz.q: What does model.compile() do in Keras?
options: Builds the layer structure of the neural network
Trains the model on the training dataset
Configures the model for training — specifying the optimiser, loss function, and evaluation metrics
Saves the trained model to a file
quiz.explanation: model.compile() = setup before training! You specify: OPTIMISER (how to update weights — e.g. Adam), LOSS FUNCTION (what to minimise — e.g. categorical_crossentropy), and METRICS (what to track — e.g. accuracy). Then model.fit() does the actual training!

quiz.q: Why do we NORMALISE input data (divide pixel values by 255) before training?
options: To reduce the file size of the images
To convert images from colour to greyscale
To scale all input values to a small range (0 to 1) so the network trains faster and more stably — large values cause unstable gradients
Because Keras only accepts values between 0 and 1
quiz.explanation: Normalisation = levelling the playing field! Raw pixel values are 0–255. Large numbers cause large gradients → unstable training. Dividing by 255 scales everything to 0–1 → smooth, stable, faster training. Always normalise before training!

options: A dataset of 70,000 labelled handwritten digit images (0–9) — the 'Hello World' of neural networks
A dataset of cat and dog photos used for image classification
A collection of text documents used for natural language processing
A financial dataset containing stock market prices
quiz.explanation: MNIST = the classic beginner dataset! 60,000 training images + 10,000 test images of handwritten digits 0–9, each 28×28 pixels. Every deep learning beginner builds a digit recogniser on MNIST — it is the 'Hello World' of neural networks!

quiz.q: What does the FLATTEN layer do in a Keras model?
options: Reduces the number of neurons in the next layer
Converts a 2D image (e.g. 28×28 pixels) into a 1D array of 784 values so it can be fed into a Dense layer
Applies a non-linear activation function to the inputs
Normalises the input data before training
quiz.explanation: Flatten = unroll the grid! A 28×28 image is a 2D grid of 784 pixels. Dense layers need 1D input. Flatten stretches the grid into a single row of 784 numbers — no information lost, just reshaped for the next layer!

quiz.q: What is the VANISHING GRADIENT problem in deep neural networks?
options: When the model's accuracy suddenly drops to zero during training
When gradients become extremely small as they flow backward through many layers — early layers learn very slowly or not at all
When the network uses too much GPU memory during training
When training data is too small for the network to learn anything
quiz.explanation: Vanishing gradients = early layers going deaf! In very deep networks using Sigmoid/Tanh, gradients shrink as they flow backwards — by the time they reach the first layers, they are nearly zero. Those layers stop learning! ReLU largely solves this problem.
